# Exodus 21: The God Who Governs Human Relationships

Exodus 21 is not a collection of abstract principles about justice. It is God speaking directly into the mess of human life—into servitude, family conflict, violence, and the thousand ways people harm one another. What becomes immediately clear is that God's law was never meant to be impersonal. He is establishing order in a community of His people, and His care extends to the most vulnerable: servants, women, children, and even slaves. The servant who serves six years and then goes free; the Hebrew maidservant whose dignity and rights her master cannot strip away; the victim of violence who deserves compensation—these laws reveal a God who sees, who remembers, and who will not permit the strong to crush the weak without consequence. This is not the God of the exploiter or the oppressor. He inserts Himself into human relations with binding, protective ordinances.

Yet this chapter should also unsettle us. The very need for these laws exposes the depth of human sinfulness. A master might drive his servant to the brink of death. A man might scheme to murder his neighbor. A father or mother might be cursed and struck by their own child. These are not hypothetical troubles; they are the recurring tragedies God knew His people would face. The precision and severity of these laws—execution for murder, retaliation "eye for eye," restitution measured in shekels—reflect how seriously God takes both human dignity and the sanctity of human life. This is not a lenient God overlooking sin; it is a God who holds His people to an exacting standard because covenant life demands it. When we read "Whoever strikes his father or mother must surely be put to death," we are encountering the weight of what it means to live in God's community. Nothing less than the preservation of fundamental human order and honor is at stake.

The principle embedded throughout this chapter is one of proportionality and protection. If your ox gores a neighbor's ox, you must divide the loss. If you strike your servant and he dies, you bear the guilt of death. But if he recovers in a day or two, you are spared—mercy is woven into the law itself. When a master injures his servant's eye or tooth, he must set the servant free. The law does not allow for a owner's absolute dominion over another human being. Even servitude, which the law permits, has boundaries God Himself enforces. A master may not reduce a wife's food, clothing, or marital rights without freeing her. Over and over, God draws a line: here is where your power ends. Here is where another person's dignity begins. This is a radical statement in any age, and it should challenge us to ask whether our own treatment of others—our employees, our family members, those who have less power than we do—reflects this God-given concern for the vulnerable.

What makes Exodus 21 ultimately significant is not that we live under these exact civil laws anymore, but that it reveals God's character and His concern for justice in human relations. Jesus would later cut to the heart of all this law and say: "Love your neighbor as yourself." But you cannot understand what that love looks like in concrete terms without seeing it in action here—in the protection of servants, the restitution demanded of the violent, the dignity God refuses to let anyone strip away. When we encounter injustice, indifference toward the vulnerable, or the rationalization of cruelty, we are seeing a world that has turned away from the God of Exodus 21. His laws stood as a wall between the weak and their would-be destroyers. We would do well to ask: do our own communities, our own decisions, our own treatment of those under our authority, reflect the God who speaks here?
